coredata.dc:title: Environmentally benign, bright luminescent carbon dots from IV bag waste and chitosan for antimicrobial and bioimaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Ramasubburayan, Ramasamy
Senthilkumar, Nangan
Kanagaraj, Kuppusamy
Basumatary, Sanjay
Kathiresan, Sellamuthu
Manjunathan, Jagadeesan
Revathi, Meyyappan
Selvaraj, Manickam
Prakash, Santhiyagu
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent carbon dots have gained significant attention in various fields due to their unique optical properties and potential applications. Here, the study was aimed to propose a novel and sustainable approach for the synthesis of luminescent carbon dots (ICDs) using IV (Intravenous) medical bag waste. The ICDs were synthesized through a facile and cost-effective method that involved the carbonization of IV bag waste followed by surface functionalization with chitosan. The synthesized ICDs were characterized using UV–Visible spectrum (UV–Vis), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-Ray Diffraction analysis (XRD), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). The size of the ICDs is between 2 and 8 nm. The ICDs effectively inhibited the growth of both gram positive and gram negative bacterial strains with the inhibitory activity in the range of 11–14 mm and 12–18 mm, respectively. Results of antibiofilm activity of ICDs varying concentrations (50 and 100 μg/ml) showed that it effectively distorted the biofilm architecture and thereby validated its promising potentials. In vitro antioxidant activity showed remarkable DPPH radical scavenging potentials of ICDs (33.4%–70.1%). Results of MTT assay revealted that ICDs showed potent cytotoxic effect on HeLa cells in a dose dependant matter (25–400 μg/ml). Furthermore, when HeLa cells were excited at wavelengths of 380 nm, 440 nm and 540 nm, cell-imaging experiments using ICDs revealed the presence of blue, green, and red fluorescence. This innovative method not only addresses the issue of IV bag waste in a sustainable manner but also opens up exciting possibilities for the advancement of versatile carbon-based materials in the field of biomedicine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Label free
IV bag waste
Chitosan
Antimicrobial
Biocompatible
Luminescence carbon dot
Bioimaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Deanship of Scientific Research, King Khalid University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University for funding this work through Large Group Research Project under grant number R.G.P. 2/354/44 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) An illustrative High-resolution TEM image shows the ICDs. (b) The histogram depicting the size distribution of the ICDs.

(a and b) The synthesized ICDs were visualized through scanning electron microscopy images (c, d and e) Elemental mapping analysis provided insights into the individual distribution of carbon(green), oxygen(purple), and nitrogen (red) within the ICDs.
The characterization of ICDs involved two main techniques: a) Analyzing X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, and b) Conducting FT-IR analysis.

a) UV–Vis absorption (red line) and fluorescence emission (blue line) spectra were utilized to analyze the optical properties of ICDs. b) The excitation dependantof fluorescent emission spectra for ICDs was investigating by varying excitation wavelengths in the range of 320–460 nm.

Antimicrobial activities on synthesized Carbon dots (ICDs) against chosen pathogenic strains; Zone of inhibition against (1) S. aureus, (2) S. mutans, (3) P. aeruginosa, (4) E. coli. a) Positive control: Streptomycin, while ICDs were tested at b) 50 μg and c) 100 μg.

Antibiofilm activity of ICDs against S. aureus and E. coli; CLSM images showed biofilms of a-c) S. aureus and d-f) E. coli cultivated for 24 h and treated with different concentrations for 12 h. Biofilms were catagorized in to (a and d) control groups, groups treated with (b and e) 50 μg/ml ICDs, and (c and f) 100 μg/ml ICDs. Live cells were depicted in green (acridine orange), and the Scale bar = 250 μm.
DPPH scavenging activity of ICDs and ascorbic acid at varying concentrations.

The viability of HeLa cells across various concentrations of ICDs, a comparative analysis was performed, employing DMSO as a reference control. The experiment was conducted in three independent analyses (n = 3).

Fluorescence images of HeLa cells stained with ICDs were captured using confocal microscopy. The images were obtained under different excitation wavelengths: (a)λex = 380 nm, (b) λex = 430 nm, (c) λex = 470 nm, (d) an overlay of the image, and (e) a three-dimensional representation of the overlayed image. The scale bar = 25 μm.

The hydrothermal technique is employed to create carbon dots from discarded IV bags, referred to as ICDs, in combination with chitosan. This synthesis method allows for their application in various fields such as antimicrobial activities and biomarker usage.
Comparative studies on PET based ICDs and their application.

row.entry: Antimicrobial, Antioxidant, Cytotoxicity and Bio Imaging

head.ce:title: Environmentally benign, bright luminescent carbon dots from IV bag waste and chitosan for antimicrobial and bioimaging applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent carbon dots have gained significant attention in various fields due to their unique optical properties and potential applications. Here, the study was aimed to propose a novel and sustainable approach for the synthesis of luminescent carbon dots (ICDs) using IV (Intravenous) medical bag waste. The ICDs were synthesized through a facile and cost-effective method that involved the carbonization of IV bag waste followed by surface functionalization with chitosan. The synthesized ICDs were characterized using UV–Visible spectrum (UV–Vis), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-Ray Diffraction analysis (XRD), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). The size of the ICDs is between 2 and 8 nm. The ICDs effectively inhibited the growth of both gram positive and gram negative bacterial strains with the inhibitory activity in the range of 11–14 mm and 12–18 mm, respectively. Results of antibiofilm activity of ICDs varying concentrations (50 and 100 μg/ml) showed that it effectively distorted the biofilm architecture and thereby validated its promising potentials. In vitro antioxidant activity showed remarkable DPPH radical scavenging potentials of ICDs (33.4%–70.1%). Results of MTT assay revealted that ICDs showed potent cytotoxic effect on HeLa cells in a dose dependant matter (25–400 μg/ml). Furthermore, when HeLa cells were excited at wavelengths of 380 nm, 440 nm and 540 nm, cell-imaging experiments using ICDs revealed the presence of blue, green, and red fluorescence. This innovative method not only addresses the issue of IV bag waste in a sustainable manner but also opens up exciting possibilities for the advancement of versatile carbon-based materials in the field of biomedicine.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), nano-sized materials with stable photoluminescence properties, have emerged as a competitive alternative to heavy-metal-containing quantum dots and rare-earth-doped nanocrystals (Liu et al., 2020). Their low toxicity (Chan et al., 2021), biocompatibility (Park et al., 2020), and favourable chemical properties have made them suitable for various applications, including drug delivery (Zeng et al., 2016), sensing (Raveendran and Kizhakayil, 2021), and optoelectronics (Stepanidenko et al., 2021). CDs are also promising for bioimaging (Kaur and Verma, 2022), and energy harvesting in luminescent solar concentrators (Zhou et al., 2018). While early CD synthesis methods involved harsh conditions, recent hydrothermal approaches offer greener and bottom-up fabrication at lower temperatures. However, the use of high-cost or toxic precursors in CD synthesis hampers scalability. To address this, researchers have explored renewable biological by-products like grass, plant leaves, apple juice, and waste paper. Nonetheless, the challenge remains to develop an industrial-scale fabrication approach for CDs due to their increasing demand (Long et al., 2021). The cost-effectiveness and environmental impact of conventional CD synthesis methods, which rely on expensive or hazardous chemicals, pose obstacles to their practical implementation and sustainability, despite their potential in bioimaging, sensing, and drug delivery.

Global demand for Poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET) has risen due to population growth and increased needs (Oladele et al., 2023). Scientists are researching sustainable methods to repurpose PET waste into carbon dots (CDs) to lessen environmental impact. These efforts aim for a circular, eco-friendly economy. Various PET utilization strategies include its use in construction materials (Mohan et al., 2021), oil spill adsorbents (Saleem et al., 2018), and water purification hydrogels (Chan and Zinchenko, 2021). Research also focuses on turning PET waste into carbon nanomaterials like carbon nanotubes and graphene (Chen et al., 2022). For instance, Hu et al. (2019) developed a low cost and greener method of producing fluorescent CDs from PET and further proved its ability to detect Fe3+ and PPi in water and urine samples, offering potential as a sensing system. Kumari and Chaudhary (2020) utilized plastic waste-derived CDs to detect E. coli, exhibiting excellent optical properties and a 60–70% quantum yield. The CDs detected E. coli in water with a sensitivity of 108 CFU/ml, while being biocompatible and non-toxic. Perikala and Bhardwaj (2022) recycled non-biodegradable plastic wastes like masks, gloves and syringes into white-light CDs through pyrolysis and embedded them in a scalable polymer phosphor. The scalable sustainable phosphor exhibited broad emission, yielding high quality white LED with CRI ∼70 and (0.25, 0.32) CIE coordinates for advanced lighting. Recently, PET waste is innovatively turned into nitrogen-doped Carbon Dots (NCDs), reducing landfilling and CO2 emissions, serving as both LED materials (48.16%) and water sensors (Ma et al., 2023). Liang et al. (2023) synthesized CDs from PET wastes through an eco-friendly approach and further validated its application in sustainable agriculture through enhanced green pea seed germination, seedling growth, and metabolic pathways.

In healthcare facilities, there is a significant amount of waste generated from IV bags, which contain carbon-rich polymers like polyethylene and polypropylene (Kibria et al., 2023). However, this waste presents an opportunity to transform it into valuable nanomaterials and reduce its environmental impact. Researchers are increasingly interested in sustainable methods for synthesizing carbon dots (CDs) using waste materials such as PET (Atchudan et al., 2021; Manzoor et al., 2023). Furthermore, chitosan derived from chitin, is well known for its antimicrobial properties and biocompatibility. These qualities make it a promising agent to enhance the functionality of CDs (Villalba-Rodríguez et al., 2022). The development of a simple and low-cost recycling process for PET waste is essential to promote sustainability and conserve both energy and resources. In this context, combination of chitosan with carbon extracted from discarded IV bags emerged as a significant method to minimize PET wastes and further explore the biomedical applications of CDs. Thus, in the present investigation an innovative and environmentally-safe method was employed to synthesize luminescent carbon dots (ICDs) from discarded IV bag waste and chitosan by optimizing factors like reaction conditions, precursor concentrations and temperature. Subsequently, the resulting ICDs were assessed for their notable antimicrobial potentials, cell imaging proficiency and biocompatibility. This method not only provides a viable way for the judicious utilization of IV bag waste but also paves the way for versatile carbon-based materials. By adopting this sustainable method, we can mitigate the environmental effects of waste and foster the development of cost-effective, functional nanomaterials for biomedical applications. The successful preparation of glowing carbon dots using IV bag remnants and chitosan offers potential in areas such as cellular visualization, antimicrobial treatments, and biofilm control. This study aims to pave the way for the eco-friendly and effective use of waste in the fabrication of functional nanomaterials, promoting advancements in biomedicine and supporting a more sustainable future.

The IV bags used in the present study were obtained from Saveetha Medical College and Hospital, Chennai, India. In order to prepare the sample, distilled water was employed as the dispersion solvent. For the antimicrobial experiments, the samples were dissolved in ethanol (Himedia, India). The test bacterial growth media Nutrient Broth and antibacterial assay medium Muller Hinton Agar was obtained from Himedia, India.

In this research, a Teflon-coated stainless-steel autoclave (50 ml) sourced from Baoshishan, India, was utilized for the hydrothermal process. The dehydrated ICDs underwent analysis using a Bruker FTIR spectrometer, sweeping a wave number range from 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1. The UV–visible spectral readings of the ICDs were captured with a V-630 Biospectrometer (JASCO) between 200 and 800 nm. The ICDs' crystalline makeup was ascertained through XRD. The physical appearance of the crafted ICDs was closely examined using SEM (Jeol JSM IT 800). The elemental breakdown of the ICDs was revealed through EDX scrutiny. Moreover, an elemental analyzer was employed to detect the amounts of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) within the ICDs.

Fluorescent ICDs were synthesized using discarded IV bags collected from Saveetha Medical College and hospital (Scheme 1 ). Briefly, the collected IV bags were cleaned thoroughly to remove the residual adhesives, impurities and other contaminants. Thereafter, the pieces of IV bags were kept in a ceramic crucible and placed inside muffle furnace. The temperature was slowly increased from room temperature to attain 400 °C at a rate of 20 °C per min. Then the oven temperature was gradually decreased at a rate of 10 °C per minute to reach ambient condition. At the end of the thermal reaction, the dark black colour powder obtained was then cooled and grounded well mechanically to obtain a powdery consistency. About 1g of the carbon powder was taken and combined with 10 ml of 3% hydrogen peroxide containing 0.2g of chitosan. The mixture was agitated thoroughly for 2 h at ambient temperature. Afterwards, it was transferred to a 100 ml Teflon-coated stainless-steel autoclave for a hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C lasting 5 h. Once cooled, the blend was passed through Whatman filter paper (0.2 μm). The resultant dark-brown carbon dot (ICDs) was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for half an hour to achieve a lucid solution. This solution underwent dialysis using a 1 KDa MWCO membrane over a span of three days. To transition the dispersed ICDs to a solid form, they were subjected to ultracentrifugation at a speed of 15,000 rpm.

Antibacterial property of the synthesized ICDs was studied through agar-well diffusion assay (Ramasubburayan et al., 2015). In brief, overnight culture of selected pathogens viz. Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus mutans, Escherichia coli, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (1 × 108 cells ml−1) was swabbed over Muller Hinton Agar (MHA) plates. Then, 100 μl containing two different concentrations 50 and 100 μg/ml of ICDs was loaded onto the wells. Positive (Streptomycin - 15 μg/ml) control was maintained separately and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. The halos (mm) formed from the edge of each well were recorded. Experiment was done in three replicates.

In order to authenticate the efficacy of carbon dots on biofilm inhibition, the selected test pathogens were allowed to form biofilms on sterilized glass piece (1 × 1 cm) both with ICDs(50 and 100 μg/ml) and without ICDs (control) and incubated for 24 hat 37 °C. Thereafter, the glass pieces were washed with sterile water containing 0.1% acridine orange and visualized by CLSM following method (Durgadevi et al., 2019).

The ability of the ICDs to quench free radical was determined by treating it with DPPH radicals (Prakash et al., 2016). In brief, an aliquot of 1 ml of varying concentrations (25–400 μg/ml) of ICDs was added to 2 ml of DPPH (0.16 mM) and vortexed for 1 min. Ascorbic acid (25–400 μg/ml) served as positive control. Both the samples were placed in dark for 30 min. Then absorbance was read at 517 nm and DPPH scavenging property was calculated as follows:
DPPH Radical scavenging (%) =(AOD - BOD) / BOD × 100
AOD: absorbance of control; BOD: absorbance of test sample.

To assess the possible toxic effects of ICDs on HeLa cell lines, we conducted an MTT assay. In brief, HeLa cells (5 × 103 cells) were first placed in a 96-well plate and then incubated at 37 °C with an atmosphere of 5% CO2 for 24 h, allowing the cells to settle and proliferate. Following this, the original medium was discarded, and the cells were introduced to a new medium infused with varying concentrations of ICDs, allowing them to be exposed to the dots for an additional 24 h. To determine the percentage of cell viability, each well was supplemented with 70 μl of untouched culture medium and 30 μL of MTT 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide, at a density of 1 mg/ml, followed by a 4-h incubation. Post incubation, each well received 0.1 ml of Dimethyl oxide (DMSO) and was left for another 10 min to dissolve the formazan crystals. The absorbance of the solutions was gauged at 540 nm using an ELISA reader, and the cell viability percentage was calculated using a designated formula.
%
of
Cell
viability = Mean
absorbance
(
t r e a t e d
s a m p l e s
u n t r e a t e d
s a m p l e s
)
x
100

Through an eco-friendly two-step process, the widely known thermoplastic polyester, PET, known for its excellent heat resistance and durability against mineral acids, oxidizing agent and sunlight was effectively converted into fluorescent ICDs. This innovative technique involves combination of air thermal oxidation and a hydrothermal procedure. Briefly, the discarded IV bags were exposed to open air and heated to 500 °C for 2h. This step lead to a series of changes in polymer and resulted in a dark brown oxidized form. The oxidized material exhibited a range of color-producing systems and an abundance of oxygen-rich groups, which made it significantly more reactive compared to the original PET. Following oxidation process, the obtained product was treated hydrothermally with chitosan at 200 °C, enabling its conversion to ICDs. The condition for synthesis was carefully optimized. Since temperature was considered as the vital factor, it was found from the experimental results that a temperature of 500 °C is more suitable for structural decomposition and final product yield (Scheme 1.). In accordance with the results of present study, several authors reported the preparation of functionalized CDs using chitin (Pawar et al., 2019); gelatin (Gu et al., 2022), polyethylene glycol (Yang et al., 2009) and polyacrylic acid (Wang et al., 2011) with varying applications.

The structural properties of the prepared ICDs were thoroughly investigated using various characterization techniques. To gain further insights, HR-TEM analysis was performed, as shown in Fig. 1 a. The results revealed that the ICDs exhibit an amorphous nature, indicating the absence of long-range order in their atomic arrangement. The SEM and TEM analysis were performed to find out the structure of ICDs. As when the ICDs were immersed in an aqueous solution, their average size was found to be between 2 and 8 nm, as depicted in Fig. 1b (Thirumalaivasan and Wu, 2020). The examination of SEM images (Fig. 2 a and b) unveiled that the ICDs are composed of aggregated structures comprising particles with a nearly spherical shape. This result is in consistent with previous study (Thulasi et al., 2020) wherein spherical shaped carbon nanomaterial with average size of 4 nm was reported from vehicle exhaust soot. In order to investigate the chemical composition and amorphous nature of the ICDs, additionally several spectroscopical analysis were performed. EDX analysis was performed to unveil the elemental composition of the prepared ICDs. The EDX mapping results, as depicted in Fig. 2c–e, clearly showcased the existence of carbon (green), oxygen (purple), and nitrogen (red) elements within the ICDs.

These elemental mapping images served as compelling evidence, confirming that the heteroatom functionalities, namely oxygen and nitrogen, were uniformly dispersed throughout the carbon framework of the ICDs. An attempt was also made to know the morphology of ICDs through XRD analysis.Fig. 3 a displayed a distinct broad peak at 2θ = 200, which suggests the presence of a graphitic carbon structure in the ICDs. This peak indicates the arrangement of carbon atoms in a layered and graphitic configuration (Shokrani Havigh and Mahmoudi Chenari, 2022).

Likewise FT-IR analysis was performed to check out the possible functional groups involved in the stabilization of ICDs. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 3b) exhibited several absorption bands, each corresponding to different vibrational modes. The peak recorded at 3352 cm−1 inferred O–H stretching vibrations, likewise the peaks noticed at 1591 and 1378 cm−1 represented vibrations due to carboxyl (CO) stretching, and O–H bending. Furthermore, asymmetric stretching of ether (C–O–C) was also noticed at 1037 cm−1. These vibrational modes provide valuable information about the functional groups present in the ICDs. In consonance with the findings of our study, the presence of a similar type of possible functional groups involved in CDs synthesis was also documented by Siddique et al. (2018).EDAX (elemental mapping) analysis was conducted to identify the possible elements. The results indicated that the synthesized ICDs predominantly consist of carbon (C), oxygen (O) and nitrogen (N). This finding strongly implies that the synthesized ICDs are composed of a carbon structure augmented with oxygen and nitrogen. By combining all the characterization data, a comprehensive understanding of the synthesized materials obtained (Zhao et al., 2019). The collective evidence confirms that the prepared materials are indeed ICDs, which possess a graphite-like structure. Additionally, the presence of a significant number of oxygen groups on the surfaces of the ICDs is evident. This characterization study gives a clear understanding regarding the structural and chemical properties of the synthesized ICDs, and thereby lays the foundation for their potential applications in various fields.

In the current study, we conducted an extensive investigation into the optical characteristics of recently developed ICDs using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Upon mixing these newly synthesized ICDs with water, we observed that the resulting solution became transparent and exhibited striking blue luminescence when exposed to UV light. Furthermore, our analysis of the UV–Vis absorption spectra of these ICDs, as depicted in Fig. 4 a (insets), indicated a prominent peak centered at approximately 320 nm, signifying the presence of n-π* transitions associated with the CO groups.

Remarkably, during our experimentation, a significant development became apparent when subjecting the aqueous solution to irradiation at 320 nm. This development was characterized by the emergence of a distinct fluorescent peak at 422 nm, clearly indicated by the presence of a blue line. Furthermore, our investigation into the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of Intrinsically Conductive Polymers (ICDs) revealed their sensitivity to the excitation wavelength, as depicted in Fig. 4b. Specifically, when excited at 320 nm, the ICDs exhibited their highest PL intensity at 422 nm. However, as we extended the range of excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 460 nm, the intensity of the emitted light gradually decreased. This change in emission behavior can be attributed to the existence of various surface emissive traps and variations in the sizes of the ICDs. In summary, our results illuminate the remarkable optical properties exhibited by the synthesized ICDs. Their ability to produce vibrant blue fluorescence when dissolved in water, along with their adaptable emission characteristics depending on the excitation wavelength, suggests their potential usefulness across diverse fields (Park et al., 2023; Venkatesan et al., 2023). These discoveries offer valuable insights and open avenues for further exploration and application of ICDs in various contexts.

In order to evaluate the impact of ICDs on the biofilm formation, we employed CLSM, imaging technique (Fig. 6 ). Here, we utilized acridine orange labelling to examine the influence of different ICDs concentrations on the permeability and disruption of biofilms. In the control group, where no ICDs treatment was administered, we observed densely formed biofilms that appeared visibly thicker upon visual inspection. However, when we exposed biofilms of S. auerus and E. coli to ICDs at concentrations of 50 μg/ml and 100 μg/ml, a significant reduction in biofilms adherence to the surface of glass substratum was noticed. Notably, as the concentration of ICDs increased, the reduction in biofilm attachment became more pronounced. To provide additional evidence of the inhibitory effects of ICDs, we conducted fluorescence intensity analysis of the biofilms. Particularly noteworthy was the significant reduction in biofilm formation observed at a concentration of 100 μg/ml ICDs, as clearly demonstrated in Fig. 6c and f. These findings underscore the remarkable capability of ICDs to effectively inhibit the formation of biofilms, offering promising prospects for combating biofilm-associated issues (Ran et al., 2019). The utilization of ICDs in biofilm imaging and research presents a valuable approach for studying the dynamics and characteristics of biofilms, paving the way for the development of novel strategies to control and prevent biofilm-related complications.

Further the potential of ICDs in scavenging free radicals was determined through an in vitro DPPH assay. The findings from the DPPH scavenging activity indicated a progressive decrease in absorbance with increasing concentrations of ICDs (Fig. 7 ). Specifically, at a concentration of 25 μg/ml, ICDs demonstrated a scavenging ability of 33.4%, whereas the positive control, ascorbic acid, exhibited a higher scavenging ability of 42.6% (Innocenzi and Stagi, 2023; Sachdev and Gopinath, 2015). Furthermore, when ICDs was tested at higher concentrations (e.g., 400 μg/ml), it exhibited a maximum DPPH radical scavenging activity of 70.1%, which was relatively lower than the scavenging ability of ascorbic acid (84.8%).Several authors efficiently demonstrated the potent antioxidant properties of carbon dots. Generally, the potential mechanism underlying the antioxidant nature of CDs depends on their ability to donate H+ ions, primarily facilitated by the presence of carboxyl, hydroxyl/amino group's surface functional groups. In this study, the decrease in absorbance of DPPH indirectly indicates that the DPPH radical is reduced by ICDs due to the uptake of H+ ion by any one of the surface groups (Murru et al., 2020; Gedda et al., 2023). Ascorbic acid (positive control) showed consistent reduction in DPPH radicals with respect to hike in concentration and thereby confirmed it to be a well-known antioxidant molecule.

In this study, the researchers aimed to evaluate the potential cytotoxic effects of ICDs by conducting an MTT assay on HeLa cells. The outcomes, depicted in Fig. 8 , displayed that even at a considerably high concentration of 1 mg/ml; the cell viability remained above 96%. This result suggests that ICDs exhibit low toxicity and a remarkable compatibility with biological systems, as reported by Liu et al. (2020). Moreover, cell imaging experiments using ICDs showed the presence of blue, green, and red fluorescence in HeLa cells when excited at wavelengths of 380 nm, 440 nm, and 540 nm, respectively, as demonstrated in Fig. 9 . These findings clearly indicate the excellent cell permeability of ICDs, pointing to their potential ashighly efficient imaging agents.

To summarize, this research presents an eco-friendly method for synthesizing ICDs from repurposed IV bags made of plastic waste. The process has piqued scientific interest for its potential environmental benefits. A comprehensive array of analytical techniques, including UV, FT-IR, and SEM, confirmed the ICDs' characteristics. These ICDs display strong fluorescence and feature –COOH and –OH groups, making them water-soluble. They have shown efficacy in inhibiting bacterial growth and biofilm formation. Importantly, the method avoids the use of costly or harmful substances and is optimized for large-scale production. Biocompatibility tests substantiated the ICDs' suitability for environmental remediation. The ICDs also exhibited excitation-dependent behavior, promising for practical applications. The study underscores the dual advantage of mitigating plastic waste and generating valuable nanomaterials.

Ramasamy Ramasubburayan: Methodology, Investigation. Nangan Senthilkumar: Writing – original draft. Kuppusamy Kanagaraj: Methodology. Sanjay Basumataray: Validation. Sellamuthu Kathiresan: Figure editions. Jagadeesan Manjunathan: Software. Meyyappan Revathi: Formal analysis. Manickam Selvaraj: Reviewing. Santhiyagu Prakash: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University for funding this work through Large Group Research Project under grant number R.G.P. 2/354/44.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Alidadykhah et al., 2022

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fuctionalization and modification of polyethylene terephthalate polymer by AgCl nanoparticles under ultrasound irradiation as bactericidal
